On the impact of atomic layer deposition processing on the chemical and opto-electrical properties of NiO thin films
Kousumi Mukherjee, $^{1, a)}$  Cristian van Helvoirt, $^{1}$  Christ H. L. Weijtens, $^{2,3}$  Marcel Verheijen, $^{1,4}$  and Mariadriana Creatore $^{1,5}$

text: Nickel oxide (NiO) is a promising p-type semiconductor extensively investigated for applications in photovoltaics, electrochemical energy storage, and gas sensors. Several deposition techniques, including solution processing, sputtering, chemical vapor deposition, and atomic layer deposition (ALD), are adopted for the synthesis of NiO films. Among these techniques, ALD is recognized for the merit of delivering pinhole-free and conformal films, relevant for processing on textured surfaces. In the present study, we have adopted three ALD NiO processes to deliver films differing in stoichiometry, crystallographic, and optoelectrical properties. To this purpose, the  $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{^tBu - MeAMD})_2$  -based thermal ALD process ("  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  ), the  $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{MeCp})_2$  -based plasma-assisted ALD (PA-ALD) process ("  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  ), and the newly developed PA-ALD process, based on the novel AlanisTM precursor ("  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  ), are compared. The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  -based process exhibits a linear growth on c-Si/ native oxide with a growth per cycle (GPC) of  $0.27\pm 0.02\AA/$  cycle at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , similar to the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  process, whereas the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  process is characterized by a higher GPC of  $0.43\pm 0.01\AA/$  cycle. The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films have the lowest Ni-to-O ratio  $(0.82\pm 0.04)$  compared to  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$ $(0.96\pm 0.05)$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$ $(0.93\pm 0.05)$ . The excess oxygen in the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films points to a larger relative content of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  acceptor states. The presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states contributes to sub-bandgap absorption, leading to a lower transmittance of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films ( $\sim79\%$ ) as compared to the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  ( $\sim84\%$ ) and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films ( $\sim90\%$ ) in the visible range. In parallel, the resistivity of the ( $\sim9\mathrm{nm}$ )  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films ( $6\pm 2\Omega\mathrm{cm}$ ) is lower than  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  ( $66\pm 33\Omega\mathrm{cm}$ ) and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films  $[(1.7\pm 0.1)\times 10^{4}\Omega\mathrm{cm}]$ , supporting the observation of the presence of a larger  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  content. Transmission electron microscopy images reveal the presence of a relatively higher density of grain boundaries in  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films that can affect carrier mobility. Furthermore, the PA-ALD NiO films exhibit a higher density ( $6.9\pm 0.8\mathrm{g/cm}^3$ ) than the thermal ALD  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films ( $5.1\pm 0.5\mathrm{g/cm}^3$ ) due to the ion flux impinging on the surface during the  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma half-cycle. Our study demonstrates that the ALD process influences the stoichiometry and microstructure of the NiO film, ultimately impacting its electrical and optical properties. Additionally, we reflect on the selection of ALD processes for specific application areas.

Nickel oxide thin films are widely investigated due to their applications, among others, in metal halide perovskite and organic solar cells, $^{1-3}$  lithium-ion batteries, $^{4,5}$  electrocatalysis, $^{6,7}$  thin film transistors, $^{8}$  gas sensors, $^{9}$  and resistivity switching devices. $^{10}$  NiO is a promising material in the realm of wide bandgap semiconductors, exhibiting a bandgap exceeding  $3.5\mathrm{eV}$ . Stoichiometric NiO is an insulator  $(>10^{13}\Omega \mathrm{cm})$ . However, as-deposited NiO exhibits p-type conductivity due to the presence of Ni vacancies or excess oxygen. $^{12}$  NiO films are also characterized by optical transmittance $^{13}$  and demonstrate an antiferromagnetic nature. $^{14}$

NiO thin films have been deposited by various methods such as solution processing,[15,16] sputtering,[17,18] spray pyrolysis,[19] chemical vapor deposition (CVD),[20,21] pulsed laser deposition,[22] and atomic layer deposition (ALD).[1,2,23] Among these deposition techniques, vapor phase deposition methods have an advantage over solution processing due to their ease of scalability. Additionally, solution processing often requires postannealing treatment, which may not be compatible with temperature-sensitive substrates. ALD distinguishes itself from the other vapor phase deposition techniques because the self-limiting nature of the surface reactions enables the growth of conformal thin films, also on textured and high surface area substrates.[24] Recent reports have shown that the adoption of ALD NiO in combination with organic hole transport layers, such as poly[bis(4-phenyl)(2,4,6-trimethylphenyl)amine or self-assembled monolayers, in tandem perovskite/c-Si [and copper indium gallium diselenide (CIGS)] devices leads to uniform coverage on textured c-Si and CIGS bottom cells, thereby suppressing shunt pathways and leading to a high yield in device performance.[25,26] Moreover, uniform coverage of thin films on 3D or porous substrates can be achieved by ALD, making it particularly advantageous for applications such as electrocatalysis.[24]

ALD processes of NiO have been developed using several Ni precursors, such as  $\mathrm{NiCp}_2$ ,[27-31]  $\mathrm{Ni(acac)}_2$ ,[14]  $\mathrm{Ni(MeCp)}_2$ ,[1,32,33]  $\mathrm{Ni(EtCp)}_2$ ,[34,35]  $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{^tBu - MeAMD})_2$ ,[25,36] etc., with different oxidants  $(\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma,  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ ,  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , or  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2)$ . Table SI in the supplementary material reports an overview of previously developed ALD NiO processes. Precursors, such as  $\mathrm{Ni(acac)}_2$ ,[37,38]  $\mathrm{Ni(thd)}_2$ ,[39,40]  $\mathrm{Ni(acac)}_2$ ,[TMEDA],[38] and  $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{^tBu}_2\mathrm{DAD})_2$ ,[41] have low volatility below  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , necessitating a higher deposition temperature, as mentioned in Table SI in the supplementary material, to promote film growth. This increases the thermal budget of the process and hampers the application on thermally sensitive substrates and surfaces.[10] To this end, selected works demonstrated ALD processes extending to lower temperatures and employing  $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{^tBu - MeAMD})_2$ ,[25]  $\mathrm{Ni(MeCp)_2}$ ,[1]  $\mathrm{Ni(EtCp)_2}$ ,[34,35] and nickel amino-alkoxides[42,43] as reported in Table SI in the supplementary material. At the same time, nickel amino-alkoxide precursors, such as  $\mathrm{Ni(dmamp)_2}$ , decompose at temperatures around  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ,[43,44] limiting the temperature window.

In the present study, we report on an ALD process based on a novel Ni precursor—Alanis™, which allows processing at a temperature as low as  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  with a temperature window of 100–250°C. Additionally, we compare the Alanis™-based plasma-assisted ALD (PA-ALD) NiO (hereafter referred to as "NiOAlanis") with two ALD NiO previously developed in our

group—namely,  $\mathrm{Ni(MeCp)_2}$ -based plasma-assisted ALD NiO and  $\mathrm{Ni(^tBu - MeAMD)_2}$ -based thermal ALD NiO (hereafter, referred to as “ $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$ ” and “ $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$ , respectively). The study includes the ALD process development of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  with saturation curves as well as a comparison with the other NiO processes in terms of processing temperature window and uniformity. Moreover, we propose a comparative study of the NiO film properties for the three processes, with the aim of demonstrating the effect of ALD processing and chemical properties on the microstructure, electrical, and optical properties. Additionally, we reflect on how specific material properties can influence the selection of certain ALD processes to fulfill requirements or optimize performance in certain application areas.
A. Atomic layer deposition of NiO

The plasma-assisted ALD process of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  is developed in a home-built ALD reactor, described elsewhere.45 It is a vacuum system equipped with a rotary and turbomolecular pumping unit that can reach a base pressure of  $10^{-6}$  Torr. The wall temperature of the reactor is kept at  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The ALD process is developed at a table temperature range of  $50 - 350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . AlanisTM, from Air Liquide, is used as the precursor, and a  $100\mathrm{W}$  inductively coupled plasma (ICP) oxygen plasma is used as the coreactant. The physical properties of the AlanisTM precursor are reported in Sec. C in the supplementary material. The AlanisTM bubbler is kept at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the precursor is vapor-drawn via a delivery line heated at  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . A constant flow of oxygen from the top of the reactor is maintained during the ALD cycle to prevent NiO deposition on the quartz tube near the ICP plasma source. The chamber pressure in the home-built ALD reactor (for both PA-ALD processes) in the presence of  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma is between  $1.4\times 10^{-2}$  and  $1.5\times 10^{-2}$  mbar. The  $\mathrm{O}_2$  flow rate is  $134\pm 5$  SCCM. For AlanisTM dosing time of 1 s or longer, the precursor is dosed in pulses with the two pulses separated by 1 s to allow for a build-up of precursor vapor pressure inside the bubbler. The vapor-drawn process, after investigation of the saturation curves, is illustrated in Fig. 1. In short, one NiO ALD cycle consists of two pulses of  $0.5\mathrm{s}$  AlanisTM dose, which is separated by 1 s, followed by 1 s of precursor purge, 5 s of  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma exposure, and 2 s of purging.

The  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  processes, along with their corresponding saturation curves, have been previously reported by Koushik et al. and Phung et al., respectively. The  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  process is carried out in the same home-built ALD reactor as adopted for the AlanisTM-based NiO process. The  $\mathrm{Ni(MeCp)_2}$  bubbler is kept at  $55^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and an argon carrier gas is used for the delivery via a delivery line heated to  $75^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The deposition is carried out at a temperature of  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the wall temperature is kept at  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  process is carried out in the commercial FlexAL MK1 ALD reactor (Oxford Instruments) because precursor saturation could not be achieved in the home-built ALD reactor, as shown in Fig. S2 in the supplementary material, due to the presence of background water. The reactor design and the uniform wall temperature of the FlexAL reactor facilitate efficient removal of residual water dose, as compared to the home-built ALD reactor. The thickness of the NiO films is determined by spectroscopic ellipsometry (SE) (Ellipsometer M2000, J.A. Woollam Co.). The growth of the ALD NiO film is monitored by in situ SE (VIS Ellipsometer M2000, J.A. Woollam Co.) on c-Si  $(100) / \sim 3\mathrm{nm}$  native oxide substrates. Additionally, the conformality of the NiO film is monitored by SE on an  $8^{\prime \prime}$  mapping stage (NIR Ellipsometer M2000, J.A. Woollam Co.). The SE data of the c-Si substrate and the native  $\mathrm{SiO}_2$  layer are modeled using the optical constants reported by Herzinger et al.[47] The NiO data are fitted by a Cauchy dispersion model in the wavelength range of  $400 - 1000\mathrm{nm}$ . The thickness nonuniformity is calculated using Eq. (1), where  $\sigma_{\mathrm{thickness}}$  and  $\overline{\mathrm{thickness}}$  is the standard deviation and average, respectively, of the thickness of the film measured at several points across the wafer and expressed as a percentage,[48]

The elemental composition of the NiO films on c-Si/native oxide substrates is analyzed by Rutherford backscattering spectroscopy (RBS) and elastic recoil detection (ERD) using a  $3.5\mathrm{MV}$  Singletron with a  $1000\mathrm{keV}$ $\mathrm{He^{+}}$  beam at an angle of  $170^{\circ}$ . Furthermore, the films are characterized by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements performed on the Thermo Scientific KA1066 spectrometer using monochromatic Al  $K_{\alpha}$  x rays with an energy of  $1486.6\mathrm{eV}$ . The data are analyzed by the AVANTAGE software with a Smart background (Shirley background with offset correction) subtraction. The binding energy axis is corrected for sample charging using the adventitious C1s peak at  $284.8\mathrm{eV}$ . The impurity content in the NiO films is evaluated after cleaning the NiO surface by  $\mathrm{Ar^{+}}$  sputtering at  $200\mathrm{eV}$  for  $10\mathrm{s}$ . The Ni-to-O ratio of the films is not reported after the surface cleaning procedure since preferential sputtering of O and reduction to metallic Ni have been observed even at low  $\mathrm{Ar^{+}}$  energies (Fig. S7 in the supplementary material).[49,50]

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is adopted to study the crystal structure of the NiO films on c-Si/native oxide substrates. The measurements are carried out in a Bruker D8 Discover using a  $\mathrm{Cu~k}_{\alpha}$ $(\lambda = 1.54\AA)$  radiation in the range of  $30^{\circ} - 80^{\circ}$  with a step size of  $0.04^{\circ}$  and a time per step of  $10\mathrm{s}$  in the grazing incidence (GI) geometry with an incidence angle of  $0.4^{\circ}$  and using two slits of 0.6 and  $0.2\mathrm{mm}$  for the incident ray. Measurements are also carried out in Bragg-Brentano geometry  $(\theta -2\theta$  scans) in the range of  $35^{\circ} - 50^{\circ}$  with a step size of  $0.4^{\circ}$ , a time per step of  $10\mathrm{s}$ , and an omega  $(\omega)$  angle offset of  $3^{\circ}$  to suppress the contribution of the Si substrate. X-ray reflectivity (XRR) measurement is also carried out in the same equipment in the range of  $0^{\circ} - 6^{\circ}$  with a step size of  $0.01^{\circ}$  and a time per step of  $0.1\mathrm{s}$ . The data are analyzed by the LEPTOS software using a model consisting of a Si wafer, a thin  $\mathrm{SiO}_2$  layer, and a crystalline NiO layer.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements are carried out in top-view on NiO films deposited on TEM windows and in cross section on Focused Ion Beam (FIB) prepared lamellas of NiO films deposited on c-Si substrates using a probe-corrected JEOL ARM 200F transmission electron microscope operated at  $200\mathrm{kV}$  and equipped with a  $100\mathrm{mm}^2$  Centurio silicon drift detector energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy detector.

The resistivity of the NiO films on the c-Si/450 nm  $\mathrm{SiO}_2$  substrate is measured at room temperature using a Signatone four-point probe in combination with a Keithley 2400 source meter. The charge carrier concentration is determined from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) by Mott-Schottky analysis. The measurement is carried out in a three-electrode electrochemical cell using a CompactStat (Ivium) potentiostat, and the data are analyzed by the IVIUM software. A. ALD NiO process with Alanis™ as precursor

The growth of the ALD  $\mathrm{NiO}_{\mathrm{Alanis}}$  film using  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma, as a coreactant, is investigated. Saturation curves for the precursor and coreactant dosing and purging steps for the  $\mathrm{Alanis}^{\mathrm{TM}}$ -based NiO process, at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , are determined from in situ spectroscopic ellipsometry and reported in Fig. 2. An  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma of  $5\mathrm{s}$  is used for the precursor saturation curve while varying the  $\mathrm{Alanis}^{\mathrm{TM}}$  dose. A soft saturation is observed for the  $\mathrm{Alanis}^{\mathrm{TM}}$  precursor dosing time [see Fig. 2(a)]. This can be due to slow kinetics at the surface, which can lead to blocked chemisorption sites becoming available as the exposure time increases.[51,52] For the  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma saturation curve [Fig. 2(c)], the precursor is dosed in pulses twice for a cumulative dosing time of  $1\mathrm{s}$ . It can be seen that there is no growth of the NiO film when the plasma is not ignited, which points to the fact that the  $\mathrm{Alanis}^{\mathrm{TM}}$  precursor does not react with molecular oxygen under the adopted ALD deposition conditions, e.g., low pressure. ALD saturating behavior is observed, within the error, for  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma half cycles with a growth per cycle (GPC) of  $0.27\pm 0.02\AA/$ cycle. A negligible variation in GPC is observed as a function of the precursor and  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma purge times, as shown in Figs. 2(b) and 2(d), respectively. A precursor and  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma purge of 1 and  $2\mathrm{s}$ , respectively, are selected for the process.

The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  process has a linear growth on c-Si without nucleation delay, as can be seen in Fig. 3(a), similar to the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  process. On the other hand, the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  process has a nucleation delay of around 50 cycles on c-Si, followed by linear growth. The delay in growth can be due to the limited number of active sites available for the chemisorption of the precursor molecule onto the substrate surface.[53-56] The GPC of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  in the

linear growth region is higher than that of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$ , as reported in Table II. The reason behind the higher GPC of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  process is discussed later. The processing time of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  process is longer than the other two processes due to the longer purge time required after the water dosing.

Next, the GPCs of the different ALD NiO processes are investigated at different temperatures, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The GPC of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  is relatively stable in the range of  $100 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . For temperatures lower than  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the GPC increases, which can be due to the condensation of the precursor, resulting in a CVD contribution. On the other hand, the increase in GPC around  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  might be due to precursor decomposition (see Fig. S3 and Table SII in the supplementary material), thereby limiting the ALD temperature window to a table temperature below  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . [57] The higher GPC of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  at low temperatures  $(50 - 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  is most likely due to a CVD growth because of the challenge of purging  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  from the chamber at low temperature. The GPC decreases with an increase in temperature, leading to a GPC of  $0.39 \pm 0.02\AA/$ cycle at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , similar to what has been reported by Hsu et al. [36] This can be attributed to the decrease in surface hydroxyl group coverage due to a thermally induced dehydroxylation reaction. [58,59] The process is not carried out at a temperature higher than  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  as the Ni  $(^{t}\mathrm{Bu - MeAMD})_2$  precursor has been reported to decompose at  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , resulting in the formation of metallic Ni. [60] The growth of  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  also decreases with an increase in temperature but remains fairly stable within the temperature window of  $150 - 300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Deposition of these NiO thin films at temperatures of  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  or lower enables processing on polyethylene terephthalate substrates, often used in flexible photovoltaics and electronics. [23,61]

The NiO film nonuniformity derived from SE mapping is compared in Fig. S4 in the supplementary material and Table II. The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  films are deposited on a  $4^{\prime \prime}$  c-Si wafer, whereas the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  layer is also deposited on an  $8^{\prime \prime}$  c-Si wafer because of the possibility of processing large area substrates in the FlexAlTM ALD reactor. The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films exhibit greater nonuniformity compared to  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  films, likely due to the soft saturation behavior of the ALD process. The area near the precursor inlet experiences a higher residence time of the precursor, leading to higher growth. This can cause a thickness gradient along the precursor flow direction, as shown in Fig. S4(a) in the supplementary material.[51,62,63]

The chemical composition of the NiO films is reported in Table III and Fig. S5 in the supplementary material. The NiO films are nonstoichiometric with an excess of oxygen content, resulting in a Ni-to-O ratio of less than 1 (reported in Table III). The presence of excess oxygen or Ni vacancies in the lattice leads to the conversion of two  $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$  ions to  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  ions to maintain charge neutrality, as shown in Fig. 4. $^{12,64,65}$  The p-type conductivity of the NiO film arises due to the formation of the  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  acceptor states. Hole transport in NiO occurs when the  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states accept a 3d electron from a neighboring  $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$  state or 2p electron from an adjacent  $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$  anion, as indicated in Fig. 4 by arrows, creating a hole in the 3d band of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$  or 2p band of  $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ , respectively. $^{65,66}$  XPS measurements are carried out to provide more information about the

The main peak at  $\sim 854\mathrm{eV}$  corresponds to Ni in its  $+2$  oxidation state, and the charge transfer  $c3d^{9}L$  state (where  $c$  stands for a hole in the Ni 2p core level and  $L$  is a hole in the O ligand orbital). This is further corroborated by the lower mass density of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films compared to the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films, as reported in Table III. The higher mass density and the lower impurity content of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films can be attributed to the film densification process occurring when ions impinge on the surface of the film during the  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma half-cycle.[73,74]

The bright field (BF) cross-sectional TEM images of the NiO films, presented in Figs. 5(d)-5(f), indicate that the layers are crystalline. In contrast, in  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films, differences in diffraction contrast reveal that some of the grains are not continuous over the entire thickness, as shown in Fig. 5(f) and Fig. S9 in the supplementary material. The crystallinity of the NiO films, as determined by GI-XRD, is shown in Fig. S11 in the supplementary material. The NiO thin films display a rock salt (face-centered cubic) crystallographic phase. All diffraction patterns show the set of (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) reflections that are expected for a poly-crystalline film although the peak ratios differ among the NiO films as well as from the powder diffraction pattern displayed as a reference. text: to the diffractogram, providing information on the out-of-plane texture of the films.[75,76] As shown in Fig. 6, the XRD diffractograms suggest a  $\langle 100\rangle$  texture for the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  films and a  $\langle 111\rangle$  texture for  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  films. The preferential growth of  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  along the  $\langle 111\rangle$  direction can be initiated by the presence of a higher content of hydroxyl groups in the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  film, which has been observed to stabilize the NiO (111) surface.[77,78] The lattice constants of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$ ,  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$ , and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  films are  $4.17\pm 0.01$ ,  $4.18\pm 0.01$ , and  $4.19\pm 0.01\mathrm{nm}$ , respectively, and agree with previous reports on NiO films.[39,79]

From an application perspective, the crystal orientation of NiO films has been reported to play a crucial role in determining their electrochemical performance, particularly in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER).[24,80] The NiO (111) surface is polar, terminating with either Ni or O atoms, whereas the NiO (200) surface is

nonpolar, terminating with both Ni and O atoms. This difference in surface termination influences the surface energy of the films. $^{3,80}$  Khamene et al. demonstrated that ALD NiO films with  $\langle 111\rangle$  texture are a more OER-active catalyst than NiO with  $\langle 200\rangle$  texture, owing to a higher hydroxide coverage and higher surface energy. The higher surface energy correlates with increased reactivity and promotes stronger interaction between the catalyst and reaction intermediates. $^{24}$  Additionally, the presence of the porous surface structure of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films can increase the accessibility of active sites for OER. $^{7}$
One of the most important material properties of NiO relevant to its many applications, particularly in solar cells, as a hole

All NiO films exhibit p-type conductivity, as evidenced by the negative slope of the Mott-Schottky plots shown in Fig. S12 in the supplementary material. $^{81}$  The lateral resistivity of the NiO films, measured by a four-point probe, is reported in Table IV. The resistivity of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  is lower than that of  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$ , being more Ni-deficient than the other NiO films.

Additionally, the charge carrier concentration of the NiO films is inferred from the Mott-Schottky analysis. As reported in Table IV, the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films exhibit the highest carrier concentration among the NiO samples, consistent with their low Ni-to-O ratio, which likely contributes to the observed low resistivity. Furthermore, the Fermi level is expected to be closer to the valence band for a p-type material as the doping level increases. The presence of a higher carrier concentration in the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  film is further corroborated by UPS measurements, shown in Figs. S14 and S17 in the supplementary material, which reveal that the Fermi level lies closer to the valence band in the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films as compared to other films.
Next to this, film morphology can affect the electrical properties, particularly mobility. The carrier mobility of the NiO films

could not be measured by the Hall effect, suggesting a low hole mobility below the measurement limit  $(1\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{Vs})$ . Based on the carrier concentration, the carrier mobility of the NiO films is estimated to be in the range of  $\sim 10^{-5}\mathrm{cm}^2\mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$  ( $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$ ) to  $10^{-3}\mathrm{cm}^2\mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$  ( $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$ ). A similar range of carrier mobility values of NiO films has been reported in the literature, varying with the synthesis route.[82,83] The lower mass density of the thermal ALD  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films indicates the presence of porosity within the films. This can negatively impact the charge carrier mobility.[84] Additionally, while the lateral grain size of the NiO films is comparable (as shown in Fig. 5),  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films, unlike  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  films, exhibit grains that do not continuously extend vertically, resulting in multiple grain boundaries. This can hinder charge transport by acting as scattering centers, thereby reducing carrier mobility.[85,86]

Looking back at the application of NiO films in organic solar cells[87] and perovskite solar cells,[88] studies have shown that lowering the resistivity can improve the device performance, particularly the fill factor (FF) and open-circuit voltage  $(\mathrm{V_{OC}})$ . Decreasing the resistivity has been shown to lead to a lower series resistance of the solar cell and lower recombination due to reduced hole accumulation at the NiO/absorber interface, thereby increasing the FF and  $\mathrm{V_{OC}}$  respectively.[87,88] However, the gain in  $\mathrm{V_{OC}}$  is modest and shadowed by the high interface recombination at the NiO-based devices due to the presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states on the surface, which have been reported to act as trap states.[3] Moreover, Boyd et al. reported that the  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  surface states can undergo a redox reaction with the organic cation of the perovskite absorber, forming a dead layer and detrimentally affecting the performance of NiO-based perovskite solar cells.[89] This highlights the complex interplay of the  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states on the resistivity and, when present at the surface, underscoring the need for tailoring NiO properties to increase device performance.

In parallel, the presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states has been observed to affect the optical properties of the NiO films.[90,91] The transmittance of the NiO films on glass decreases in the visible region (400–800 nm) upon a decrease in NiO film resistivity, with the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  having the highest transmittance, as shown in Fig. 7(a). The lower transmittance and resistivity of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  film compared to the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  films can be attributed to the precursor rather than the difference in the plasma exposure time during the  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma half-cycle. To assess the impact of the total plasma exposure,  $\mathrm{Alanis}^{\mathrm{TM}}$ -based NiO films are also deposited using  $3\mathrm{s}\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma exposure, similar to the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  process. These films exhibit resistivity, transmittance (Fig. S18 in the supplementary material), film density, and relative impurity content, similar to the films deposited with  $5\mathrm{s}\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma exposure

text: (see Table SIII in the supplementary material). The decrease in transmittance of  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  films is mainly due to the absorption by these films in the visible spectrum because the reflection by all the NiO films is similar, as shown in Fig. S15 in the supplementary material. This is supported by the continuous background absorption in the order of nearly  $\sim 10^{5}\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$ , as compared to  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$ , in the visible spectrum [Fig. 7(b)]. Miedzinska et al. attributed the reduced transmittance of nonstoichiometric NiO to the presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  acceptor states and hydroxides, leading to the formation of defect states near the valence band edge.[90,92] While transitions from these defect states are typically forbidden, they occur due to phonon interaction, resulting in low-intensity sub-bandgap absorption. These defect states exhibit several transitions with energies ranging from 1.1 to  $3.5\mathrm{eV}$ , leading to a continuous background absorption.[64,90-92] To conclude, a transmittance loss of  $\sim 11\%$  in the wavelength range of  $400 - 800\mathrm{nm}$  is observed for  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  compared to  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$  on lowering the resistivity by a factor of  $\sim 10^{4}$ . When employed as a hole transport layer in p-i-n perovskite solar cells, a decrease in the transmittance of NiO can affect the short-circuit current density ( $\mathrm{J_{SC}}$ ). For instance, Zhang et al. observed that increasing the transmittance of the NiO film from  $60\%$  to  $80\%$  in the visible region increased the  $\mathrm{J_{SC}}$  by  $\sim 2\mathrm{mA/cm}^2$ .[93]

The optical bandgap  $\left(\mathrm{E_g}\right)$  of the NiO films is calculated from the Tauc plot using the absorption coefficient derived from UV-visible measurements, as shown in Fig. S16 in the supplementary material. The band structure of stoichiometric NiO, as determined by density functional theory calculations, reveals that it has an indirect bandgap,[94] but the presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  acceptor states near the valence band maxima results in a direct optical transition.[95] In this study, the direct bandgap of the NiO films is

estimated from the steep absorption onset in the Tauc plots (see Fig. S16 in the supplementary material). Since the NiO films have sub-bandgap absorption, the bandgap is estimated from the intersection of the Tauc line with a baseline fitting the sub-bandgap absorption region.[96,97] As can be seen in Table IV, the optical bandgap decreases with a decrease in resistivity, with the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  having a wider bandgap than  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$ . Wrobel et al. demonstrated that hole doping alters the electronic band structure of NiO, leading to the narrowing of the bandgap as the dopant concentration increases.[64,98]

Figure S17 in the supplementary material presents a schematic energy-level diagram of the NiO films. The valence band edge aligns well with many commonly adopted perovskite $^{99}$  and organic absorbers, $^{100}$  facilitating efficient hole extraction, while the conduction band is sufficiently high to block electron transfer. Nevertheless, band alignment also depends on the chemical composition of the absorber, and some absorbers, such as certain narrow bandgap perovskites with high valence band maximum, may not align well. $^{101}$  This indicates that all the ALD NiO films are ideally suitable as hole transport layers in organic and perovskite solar cells, as well as in tandem photovoltaics. However, as discussed earlier, the presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states at the NiO surface may negatively impact the device performance.

This study presents a comprehensive comparison of NiO films from three ALD processing routes, including a newly developed PA-ALD process of NiO using the novel Alanis™ precursor. The  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  process exhibits a linear growth with a GPC of  $0.27 \pm 0.02\AA/$ cycle in the temperature window of  $100 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  process has a similar GPC, whereas the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu-MeAMD}}$
Journal of Vacuum Science & Technology A
J. Vac. Sci. Technol. A 43(6) Nov/Dec 2025; doi: 10.1116/6.0004826

process is characterized by a higher GPC of  $0.43 \pm 0.01 \AA/$ cycle at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The presence of a relatively larger impurity content due to the residual precursor ligands can lead to the observed higher GPC value. Furthermore, the presence of energetic ions from the  $\mathrm{O}_2$  plasma impinging on the NiO surface leads to a denser PA-ALD NiO film, as compared to the thermal  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MEAMD}}$  films.

All the NiO films are substoichiometric in nature, with the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  film having the lowest Ni-to-O ratio, pointing to a higher relative content of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  acceptor states. Moreover, the higher H content in the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  film, compared to the PA-ALD NiO films, can also lower the  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  content in the former film. This results in a lower resistivity of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  film as compared to  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$ . Furthermore, the presence of porosity, as suggested by the lower density of the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films and a relatively higher density of grain boundaries, can affect the carrier mobility, thereby leading to the high resistivity observed in the films. On the other hand, the presence of a relatively larger  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  content in the  $\mathrm{NiO_{Alanis}}$  leads to absorption in the visible region due to sub-bandgap absorption by the  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states and a lower transmittance as compared to  $\mathrm{NiO_{MeCp}}$  and  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$ . To conclude, the ALD process affects the morphology and chemical composition of the NiO films, thereby influencing the optical and electronic properties of the NiO films.

Also, reflecting on the properties of NiO films in the context of their applications, it is worth reporting that the presence of  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states not only affects the film's optoelectrical properties but also impacts the implementation of NiO films in devices. For example,  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states on the surface of NiO films, serving as hole transport layers, can lead to trap-assisted nonradiative recombination and redox reactions at the NiO/perovskite absorber interface. This suggests that NiO films with a lower Ni-to-O ratio, such as  $\mathrm{NiO}_{\mathrm{Alanis}}$ , may exhibit a lower performance when implemented as a hole transport layer, as compared to devices based on the other NiO films. Beyond  $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$  states, the crystallographic orientation of NiO films can also influence their performance in specific applications, such as electrocatalysis. Notably, NiO films exhibiting a  $\langle 111\rangle$  texture, such as  $\mathrm{NiO_{Bu - MeAMD}}$  films, demonstrate a higher OER activity compared to NiO films with a  $\langle 200\rangle$  texture due to their higher hydroxide coverage and higher surface energy. These findings underscore the need for the tailoring of NiO material properties, with careful consideration of their implications, to optimize performance in specific applications.
